attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to her General Practice clinic at 34 weeks gestation with a five-day history of an intensely itchy rash. She is primigravida and has had an uncomplicated pregnancy thus far. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy multivitamins. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The rash consists of erythematous, urticarial papules and plaques starting within the striae on her abdomen and spreading to her thighs and buttocks. The rash spares her umbilicus, breasts, face, palms, and soles.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Pruritic urticarial papules and plaques of pregnancy
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation described is most consistent with pruritic urticarial papules and plaques of pregnancy (PUPPP), also known as polymorphic eruption of pregnancy. PUPPP typically presents in the third trimester of pregnancy, particularly in primigravida women, with an intensely pruritic rash that starts within abdominal striae and spreads to the thighs and buttocks, sparing the umbilicus, face, palms, and soles.

Pemphigoid gestationis is an autoimmune blistering disorder that can occur during pregnancy. While it also presents with pruritus, it typically starts with urticarial plaques around the umbilicus and progresses to tense blisters. The sparing of the umbilicus in this case makes pemphigoid gestationis less likely.

Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy presents with intense pruritus, typically starting on the palms and soles, without a visible rash. It is associated with elevated bile acids and liver enzymes. The presence of a visible rash in this case makes intrahepatic cholestasis unlikely.

Atopic eruption of pregnancy is a term encompassing eczematous eruptions that occur during pregnancy. It typically presents earlier in pregnancy and often affects women with a history of atopy. The presentation and timing in this case are more consistent with PUPPP.

Impetigo herpetiformis is a rare dermatosis of pregnancy that presents with widespread erythematous plaques studded with sterile pustules, often accompanied by systemic symptoms. The absence of pustules and systemic symptoms makes this diagnosis unlikely.

PUPPP is a clinical diagnosis and does not require specific investigations for confirmation. It is a self-limiting condition that typically resolves within days to weeks after delivery. Management is primarily symptomatic, including topical corticosteroids for pruritus and oral antihistamines if needed. Reassurance about the benign nature of the condition and its lack of adverse effects on the fetus is important.
learningPoints: Pruritic urticarial papules and plaques of pregnancy (PUPPP) typically presents in the third trimester with an intensely pruritic rash starting on the abdomen.
PUPPP is a clinical diagnosis and does not require specific investigations for confirmation.
PUPPP is a self-limiting condition that resolves after delivery but can be managed symptomatically with topical corticosteroids and oral antihistamines.
references: Kurien G, Carlson K, Badri T. Dermatoses of Pregnancy. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 [updated 2024 Jan 11]. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK570853/
